title: IFRC Humanitarian Diplomacy Internship 2026 in New York, USA | Paid Internship ($110/Day) for Graduate Students
summary: Are you passionate about humanitarian affairs, international relations, diplomacy, disaster response, and global policy? The International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies (IFRC) has announced applications for its Humanitarian Diplomacy Internship 2026 , offering an exceptional opportunity for graduate students and recent graduates to gain hands-on experience at the United Nations Headquarters in New York. This paid internship allows participants to work directly with one of the world s largest humanitarian organizations while contributing to humanitarian diplomacy, policy research, international advocacy, and global humanitarian initiatives. Interns will receive a stipend of USD $110 per day , gain exposure to the United Nations system, and build valuable professional networks within the humanitarian and international development sectors. If you aspire to build a career with the United Nations, humanitarian organizations, international NGOs, or diplomatic institutions, this internship provides practical experience that will strengthen your professional profile. About the International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies (IFRC) The International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies (IFRC) is the world s largest humanitarian network. Working together with: 191 National Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies More than 16.5 million active volunteers Humanitarian operations reaching approximately 160 million people worldwide The IFRC works to improve the lives of vulnerable people through disaster response, health services, climate resilience, migration support, humanitarian diplomacy, community development, and emergency preparedness. Its Delegation to the United Nations serves as the organization s official representative at UN Headquarters and works closely with: UN Member States UN Agencies International partners Governments Humanitarian organizations The delegation also advocates for humanitarian priorities and ensures vulnerable populations remain at the center of global policy discussions. Internship Overview Position: Intern – Humanitarian Diplomacy Organization: International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies (IFRC) Location: New York City, New York, USA Work Format: On-site Internship Duration: Start Date: August 31, 2026 End Date: December 31, 2026 Minimum Commitment: 2–3 working days per week Minimum duration of four months Possible extension depending on organizational needs Compensation: USD $110 per day Paid twice every month Please note that internship payments are taxable unless exempt under applicable double-taxation agreements. What Will Interns Gain? Successful applicants will have the opportunity to: Develop practical experience in humanitarian diplomacy. Understand how the United Nations operates in practice. Learn how international humanitarian policy is developed. Gain exposure to global humanitarian negotiations. Contribute to IFRC representation at the United Nations. Strengthen expertise in disaster management, migration, health, humanitarian affairs, and international relations. Build professional networks within the international humanitarian community. Improve research, communication, policy analysis, and advocacy skills. Internship Responsibilities Interns will support the IFRC Delegation to the United Nations through a wide range of responsibilities. 1. Monitor UN Meetings and Processes Interns will: Follow discussions at the UN General Assembly. Observe meetings of the Economic and Social Council. Monitor Security Council discussions. Prepare summaries and action points for IFRC teams. Key policy topics include: Humanitarian coordination Global partnerships Disaster risk reduction Public health Migration Protection of vulnerable populations 2. Research and Policy Analysis Interns will: Conduct research on humanitarian issues. Prepare analytical reports. Support policy development. Monitor emerging humanitarian challenges. 3. Draft Official Statements Participants will assist with: Preparing statements delivered to the UN General Assembly. Editing policy documents. Reviewing humanitarian messaging. Supporting advocacy materials. 4. Support Humanitarian Negotiations Interns will: Observe UN negotiation processes. Track developments in humanitarian resolutions. Support advocacy efforts aimed at advancing humanitarian priorities. 5. Communications and Administrative Support Responsibilities include: Creating and maintaining databases. Drafting correspondence. Supporting digital communications. Processing information requests. Assisting with daily administrative tasks. 6. Uphold Humanitarian Principles Interns are expected to work in accordance with the Fundamental Principles of the Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement throughout the internship. Eligibility Requirements Applicants should: Be currently enrolled in graduate studies or have recently graduated. Study or have studied: International Relations Humanitarian Assistance Political Science Or another closely related discipline. Demonstrate strong interest in: Humanitarian action International development Global policy International relations. Possess excellent written and verbal communication skills. Have experience with communications and social media. Be able to explain complex issues clearly. Demonstrate attention to detail. Work effectively in small teams. Be flexible, organized, and capable of working independently. Visa Requirements Applicants must already possess: A valid U.S. visa A valid U.S. Social Security Number The IFRC does not provide visa sponsorship or visa assistance. Applicants are responsible for: Travel expenses Accommodation Living costs Visa arrangements Internship Benefits Selected interns will enjoy numerous professional benefits, including: Paid internship at USD $110 per day. Real-world experience working with the United Nations. Exposure to humanitarian diplomacy. Professional mentorship. International networking opportunities. Hands-on policy and advocacy experience. Enhanced research and communication skills. Opportunity to contribute to global humanitarian initiatives. Potential internship extension. How to Apply Applicants must submit: Resume (CV) Cover Letter The cover letter should clearly indicate: Number of days available per week Desired internship duration Applications should be submitted via email to: Newyork.delegation@ifrc.org Only complete applications will be considered. Application Deadline Deadline: July 7, 2026 (11:59 PM Eastern Time) Late or incomplete applications will not be accepted. Why You Should Apply The IFRC Humanitarian Diplomacy Internship is an outstanding opportunity for graduate students interested in humanitarian affairs, diplomacy, international policy, disaster management, and global development. Working alongside experienced humanitarian professionals at the United Nations offers valuable exposure that can open doors to future careers with international organizations, NGOs, governments, and multilateral institutions. If you are committed to humanitarian action and aspire to make a global impact, this internship provides the knowledge, practical experience, and international exposure needed to advance your career. For more information, visit the official website Discover more global paid internship on OFY, VISIT HERE The post IFRC Humanitarian Diplomacy Internship 2026 in New York, USA | Paid Internship ($110/Day) for Graduate Students appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: Mediterranean Futures Residency 2026 in Tunisia | Fully Funded International Residency for Artists, Designers & Creative Technologists
summary: The Mediterranean Futures Residency 2026 is now officially open for applications, offering an exceptional fully funded international residency opportunity for artists, designers, architects, researchers, and creative technologists interested in exploring the intersection of cultural heritage, contemporary art, memory, and emerging technologies. Organized by EL SPACE in Tunisia, this prestigious two-month residency will take place from October 1 to November 30, 2026, in the culturally significant regions of Tunis, Sidi Bou Said, and Carthage. The residency is designed to support experimental artistic research, interdisciplinary collaboration, and critical inquiry into how technology is reshaping the understanding, preservation, and future of heritage. A select cohort of only five international residents will be chosen through a competitive global open call. About the Mediterranean Futures Residency 2026 The Mediterranean Futures Residency is an ambitious international initiative that brings together practitioners from across disciplines to investigate how heritage evolves in an age shaped by digital transformation, artificial intelligence, technological mediation, and changing cultural narratives. Rather than treating heritage as a static archive of the past, the residency encourages participants to examine heritage as an evolving, contested, and future-oriented field of cultural production. Residents will engage with: Cultural memory Architecture and archaeology Archives and counter-archives Oral histories and storytelling Artificial intelligence and heritage Digital fabrication Contemporary artistic practice Sound, image, and technological experimentation Climate and environmental impacts on cultural identity Decolonial narratives and networked culture The program prioritizes experimentation, dialogue, speculative thinking, and process-driven research over polished exhibition-ready outputs. Residency Location: Tunisia’s Historic and Cultural Landscape The residency is situated within one of the Mediterranean region’s most historically rich environments. Participants will be based in Tunis and work extensively in: Sidi Bou Said Carthage Ennejma Ezzahra Palace EL FABSPACE Lac EL SPACE Creative Hub Residents will have opportunities to immerse themselves in Tunisia’s layered Mediterranean, African, and Arab cultural histories while gaining access to contemporary digital fabrication tools and innovation infrastructure. The residency creates a unique bridge between historical knowledge systems and emerging technologies. Curatorial Focus of the Residency The curatorial framework centers on the idea that heritage is not simply inherited from the past but actively constructed, interpreted, and transformed through contemporary culture and technological shifts. Participants are encouraged to critically explore questions such as: How do digital technologies reshape collective memory? What happens when AI interacts with archives and historical narratives? How can artists reinterpret architecture, craft traditions, and material culture? What role does technology play in preserving or challenging heritage? How can artistic practice create new relationships between history and future societies? The residency strongly supports transdisciplinary and experimental approaches. Areas of Interest Applications may focus on a broad range of themes, including but not limited to: Cultural Historical Inquiry Cultural memory and collective identity Oral histories and storytelling Public space and contested histories Decolonial historical narratives Archives and counter-archives Art, Architecture Spatial Practice Architecture and spatial heritage Urbanism and heritage preservation Material culture and craftsmanship Contemporary artistic interpretations of heritage Technology Innovation Artificial intelligence and memory Digital heritage and preservation Interactive and digital works Digital fabrication and prototyping Technological mediation through sound and image Environmental Future-Oriented Research Climate change impacts on local cultures Speculative futures of heritage Networked culture and emerging technologies Who Can Apply? The Mediterranean Futures Residency welcomes both emerging and established practitioners from diverse disciplines. Eligible applicants include: Visual artists Photographers Video artists Sound artists Designers Architects Digital and new media artists Creative technologists Independent researchers Scholars Hybrid and interdisciplinary practitioners Applicants should demonstrate: A strong artistic or research practice Conceptual clarity Critical engagement with heritage and technology A compelling project proposal Interest in interdisciplinary collaboration What the Residency Covers Selected residents will receive a comprehensive support package, including: Fully Funded Accommodation Participants will receive full accommodation throughout the two-month residency in Tunis. Travel Support The program provides travel assistance to and from Tunisia. Meal Support Residents will receive meal support during the residency period. Production Fabrication Support Participants will gain access to advanced digital fabrication facilities, including: 3D printing Laser cutting Digital fabrication equipment Prototyping resources Mentorship Research Guidance Residents will receive curatorial support and mentorship from invited specialists, researchers, and practitioners. Institutional Archival Access Participants will have opportunities to engage with: Cultural institutions Heritage archives Historical collections Creative communities International Cohort Experience Residents will collaborate within a highly selective international interdisciplinary cohort. Public Presentation Opportunities The residency culminates in public presentations, exhibitions, conversations, and critical dialogues. Residency Philosophy The Mediterranean Futures Residency is grounded in the belief that artistic practice is a unique form of knowledge production capable of generating new understandings of culture, history, and collective life. The residency emphasizes: Research-based artistic inquiry Intellectual experimentation Speculative thinking Process-oriented work Interdisciplinary exchange Creative risk-taking Participants are encouraged to pursue ambitious and unresolved ideas rather than finalized commercial projects. Possible Residency Outcomes Projects developed during the residency may include: Video and moving-image works Sound installations and compositions Digital and interactive artworks Experimental archives Fabricated objects and prototypes Hybrid artistic formats Research-based installations Public Program International Exchange Throughout the residency, participants will contribute to: Studio visits Public talks Artist presentations Informal exchanges Critical discussions Collaborative workshops The residency will also host invited curators, theorists, architects, and cultural practitioners from across the Mediterranean region and beyond. Selection Process Only five residents will be selected through an international review process conducted by an Advisory Committee. Applications will be evaluated based on: Artistic excellence Conceptual originality Relevance to the curatorial framework Research quality Feasibility of the proposed project Potential contribution to the cohort Engagement with Tunisia’s cultural context Shortlisted applicants may be invited for an interview or conversation before final selection. Application Requirements Applicants must submit all materials in English or French. Required documents include: Curriculum Vitae (maximum 3 pages) Artist Biography (maximum 300 words) Statement of Practice (maximum 500 words) Project Proposal (maximum 1,000 words) Portfolio (up to 10 works) Preliminary Production Resources Plan Optional links to websites, videos, or online work All application materials must be submitted as PDF files. Application Deadline Application Deadline: July 19, 2026 (12 PM Tunis Time) Residency Dates Notification of Results: July 30, 2026 Residency Period: October 1 – November 30, 2026 About EL SPACE EL SPACE is Tunisia’s first social innovation hub dedicated to empowering communities through creativity, innovation, and interdisciplinary collaboration. Founded in 2015 following Tunisia’s democratic transition, EL SPACE has grown into one of North Africa’s most dynamic creative ecosystems. Its network includes: EL SPACE Lafayette A coworking and civic innovation hub in downtown Tunis. EL FABSPACE Lac A digital fabrication laboratory equipped with: 3D printers Laser cutters PCB milling machines 3D scanners Creative Hub Sidi Bou Said A cultural innovation space located within the historic Ennejma Ezzahra Palace dedicated to art, craftsmanship, and design. The Mediterranean Futures Residency combines all these spaces into a singular environment where historical heritage meets future-oriented artistic experimentation. Applicants interested in contemporary art, digital culture, heritage research, speculative design, and interdisciplinary collaboration are strongly encouraged to apply. How to Apply Applications must be submitted via email to: aslema@elspace.co Click HERE to learn more about the Mediterranean Futures Residency 2026 in Tunisia. Explore more opportunities on OFY HERE . The post Mediterranean Futures Residency 2026 in Tunisia | Fully Funded International Residency for Artists, Designers Creative Technologists appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: WIC Global Youth Leaders Program 2026: Fully Funded Opportunity for Emerging Digital, AI, and Internet Leaders Worldwide
summary: Applications Open for the World Internet Conference Global Youth Leadership Program (GYLP) 2026 Young innovators, digital leaders, internet governance experts, technology entrepreneurs, researchers, policymakers, and changemakers from around the world now have an exciting opportunity to join one of the most influential international youth leadership initiatives in the digital sector. The World Internet Conference (WIC) has officially opened applications for the 2026 Global Youth Leadership Program (GYLP), a prestigious global platform designed to identify, connect, and empower the next generation of leaders shaping the future of the internet, artificial intelligence, digital governance, and technological innovation. Selected Global Youth Leaders will gain access to international forums, high-level networking opportunities, leadership development activities, research collaborations, and the opportunity to attend and speak at the renowned World Internet Conference Wuzhen Summit in China. Applications are open until July 24, 2026. About the World Internet Conference (WIC) The World Internet Conference has become one of the world s most influential international platforms for dialogue, cooperation, and governance in cyberspace. Since the inaugural Wuzhen Summit in 2014, the conference has successfully convened global leaders from: Governments International organizations Technology companies Academic institutions Research organizations Industry associations Civil society organizations Each year, more than 1,000 high-level representatives gather to discuss critical issues surrounding: Internet governance Artificial intelligence Digital transformation Cybersecurity Digital economy development International technological cooperation In July 2022, the World Internet Conference was formally established as an international organization headquartered in Beijing, China. The organization seeks to promote: Global digital cooperation Shared internet governance Inclusive technological development International collaboration Digital innovation Sustainable cyberspace development What is the Global Youth Leadership Program (GYLP)? Launched in 2023, the Global Youth Leadership Program is one of the flagship initiatives of the World Internet Conference. The program identifies outstanding young professionals and leaders working across internet-related fields and provides them with opportunities to contribute to global discussions about the future of technology and digital governance. Over the past three years: 56 Global Youth Leaders from 38 countries and regions have participated. Participants have attended the Wuzhen Summit Youth Forum. Youth leaders have engaged directly with some of the most influential figures in global technology and internet governance. The 2025 program attracted applicants from nearly 120 countries and regions. The initiative aims to create a long-term international network of youth leaders capable of driving positive digital transformation worldwide. Core Pillars of the Program 1. Forum Participants will take part in the Global Youth Leadership Forum during the WIC Wuzhen Summit. The forum provides opportunities to: Speak on major internet issues. Share innovative perspectives. Engage with global experts. Influence discussions on digital governance. 2. Training The program organizes leadership training and field research opportunities around the world. Participants benefit from: Expert lectures Leadership development International study visits Skills enhancement programs Global networking opportunities 3. Research Youth leaders contribute to research initiatives focused on: Internet governance Artificial intelligence Digital technology Digital economy Internet policy Emerging technologies Research outcomes may contribute to international discussions, policy recommendations, and governance frameworks. Benefits of Becoming a WIC Global Youth Leader Selected participants receive a wide range of benefits. Official Recognition Participants receive an official Global Youth Leader Certificate issued by the World Internet Conference. Attend the 2026 WIC Wuzhen Summit Global Youth Leaders will be invited to: Attend the summit as official guests. Speak at the Youth Forum. Engage directly with international leaders. Travel Support Eligible participants may receive support covering: Round-trip airfare Accommodation Meals and catering Global Networking Participants gain access to high-level networking opportunities with: Government representatives International organizations Technology executives Academic experts Researchers Industry leaders Leadership Opportunities Global Youth Leaders may also: Organize workshops and side events. Participate in future WIC activities. Lead youth initiatives. Build international collaborations. Career Development Participants receive: Priority consideration for WIC internships. Priority access to job opportunities within the organization. Youth Community Membership Outstanding finalists who are not ultimately selected still receive: Nominee Certificates Access to the WIC Youth Community Networking opportunities Priority internship access Eligibility Criteria Applicants must: Support the mission and vision of the World Internet Conference. Be under 45 years of age. Demonstrate achievements in internet-related fields. Possess an international perspective. Show interdisciplinary thinking. Have strong communication and presentation skills. Preferred Professional Backgrounds Policy and Governance Applicants may be: Government officials Policy analysts Digital governance experts International organization professionals Business and Entrepreneurship Applicants may be: Startup founders Technology entrepreneurs Digital business leaders Innovation professionals Research and Academia Applicants may have expertise in: Artificial Intelligence Big Data Internet Governance Emerging Technologies Digital Policy Public Interest and Civil Society Applicants may work in: NGOs Digital inclusion initiatives Technology-for-good projects Sustainable development programs Application Requirements Applicants must submit: 1. Recommendation Letter A recommendation from: A professional institution Academic institution Recognized industry expert 2. Supporting Documents Examples include: Awards Publications Research outputs Patents Professional achievements 3. Video Submission Applicants must submit: A video presentation Written transcript The video should discuss: Views on the WIC Global Youth Leaders Initiative Proposed activities or projects Vision for digital development and internet governance APPLY HERE Important Dates Application Deadline July 24, 2026 17:00 (GMT+8) Review Process By the end of August 2026 Selection Notifications By the end of September 2026 Wuzhen Summit Participation Tentatively scheduled for November 2026 Visit HERE to learn more about the WIC Global Youth Leaders Program 2026 Additional Opportunity: Global Logo Design Competition Alongside the fellowship application, WIC is also inviting global creators to design the official logo of the Global Youth Leadership Program. Who Can Participate? Anyone worldwide. Submission Deadline July 31, 2026 17:00 (UTC+8) Benefits for Selected Designers Global recognition Official adoption of the logo Commemorative gifts Invitation to attend the 2026 WIC Wuzhen Summit Youth Forum This provides a unique opportunity for designers and creative professionals to contribute to a major international youth initiative. Click HERE to read more and apply for the Global call for logo designs for WIC Global Youth Leadership Program Why You Should Apply The WIC Global Youth Leadership Program stands out as one of the most prestigious global youth leadership initiatives focused on internet governance, AI, digital transformation, and technology policy. Participants gain: International visibility Global networking Leadership development Speaking opportunities Research collaboration Career advancement Access to influential digital governance discussions For young leaders committed to shaping the future of technology and the internet, this program offers a rare opportunity to engage directly with decision-makers and contribute to global conversations that will define the digital age. Conclusion As artificial intelligence, digital governance, cybersecurity, and internet technologies continue to reshape societies worldwide, the need for globally minded youth leaders has never been greater. The World Internet Conference Global Youth Leadership Program 2026 provides a powerful platform for emerging leaders to engage with policymakers, researchers, entrepreneurs, and innovators while helping shape the future of cyberspace. If you are passionate about technology, governance, innovation, AI, digital inclusion, or internet policy, this fully funded opportunity could be your gateway to becoming part of a global network of future digital leaders. Interested applicants are encouraged to prepare their materials early and submit their applications before the July 24, 2026 deadline. Discover more global Fellowship , Conference and Leadership Development opportunities on OFY. title: WHO African Region Opens Applications for 2026 Roster of Consultants on NTD Health Systems Integration
summary: The World Health Organization (WHO) African Region has officially opened applications for its 2026 Roster of Consultants on Neglected Tropical Diseases (NTD) Health Systems Integration. This prestigious consultancy opportunity is designed for experienced public health and health systems professionals who are passionate about strengthening healthcare systems and advancing sustainable disease control strategies across Africa. The consultancy roster will support the WHO Regional Office for Africa through the Expanded Special Project for Elimination of Neglected Tropical Diseases (ESPEN). Selected experts may be called upon to provide technical assistance to countries across multiple domains including health financing, supply chain systems, integrated service delivery, digital health systems, surveillance, and health information management. This is a major opportunity for global public health professionals, consultants, epidemiologists, health informatics experts, supply chain specialists, and health systems strengthening practitioners who want to contribute to large-scale health transformation initiatives in low- and middle-income countries. About WHO ESPEN and the Initiative The Expanded Special Project for Elimination of Neglected Tropical Diseases (ESPEN) is part of the Disease Prevention and Control Cluster within the WHO African Region. ESPEN works to help African countries achieve the goals of the WHO NTD Roadmap 2021–2030 by supporting sustainable and country-led approaches to eliminating neglected tropical diseases. The initiative focuses heavily on: Strengthening national health systems Improving integrated disease control strategies Enhancing surveillance systems Supporting health financing sustainability Integrating NTD programmes into routine healthcare systems Promoting data-driven decision-making Supporting community-centered healthcare interventions As countries move away from standalone disease campaigns toward integrated national health systems, WHO is now seeking highly qualified consultants who can support this transition. Purpose of the Consultant Roster The main purpose of the roster is to create a pool of pre-qualified technical experts who can be rapidly deployed to support WHO Country Offices and national governments across Africa. Consultants may support: Country readiness assessments Health systems integration planning Technical implementation support Monitoring and evaluation activities Documentation of country experiences and best practices Development of implementation tools and frameworks Capacity building and technical training Disease surveillance strengthening Health financing and sustainability planning Being included in the roster does not guarantee immediate employment, but it allows WHO to mobilize consultants quickly when country-specific assignments become available. Areas of Technical Expertise Required WHO is seeking experts across several specialized domains. 1. Supply Chain and Medicines Management Consultants in this area may support: Integration of preventive chemotherapy medicines into national supply chain systems Alignment with national medicines policies and essential medicines lists Integration with electronic logistics management information systems (eLMIS) Monitoring stock management, wastage, expiry tracking, and reconciliation systems Strengthening campaign logistics and last-mile distribution systems 2. Health Information Systems and Digital Integration WHO is particularly interested in professionals experienced in: DHIS2 implementation and optimization National HMIS integration Digital health interoperability Data governance systems Mapping NTD data flows within national health systems Alignment of NTD reporting systems with national digital health strategies Consultants may work directly with Ministries of Health and WHO Country Offices to strengthen national digital health architecture. 3. Integrated Service Delivery Experts may support: Integrated health campaign planning School-based and community-based service delivery models Primary healthcare integration Transition from campaign-driven models to routine healthcare delivery systems Multi-disease service delivery approaches 4. Integrated Disease Surveillance This technical area focuses on: Integration of NTD surveillance into Integrated Disease Surveillance and Response (IDSR) systems Post-elimination surveillance frameworks Sentinel surveillance systems Use of routine health data for outbreak response and disease monitoring 5. Health Financing and Sustainability Consultants may contribute to: National budgeting and planning integration Domestic resource mobilization strategies Sustainability planning for NTD interventions Donor transition strategies Financial analysis and policy development Typical Consultancy Assignments Selected consultants may be engaged in a wide range of short-term technical assignments including: Country readiness assessments Multi-country technical reviews Health systems mapping exercises Development of implementation roadmaps Capacity-building workshops Technical consultations and validation meetings Policy analysis and strategic planning Development of operational tools and frameworks Data analysis and reporting Knowledge product development Documentation of lessons learned and best practices Assignments may be remote, in-country, or hybrid depending on programme requirements. Required Qualifications Educational Requirements Applicants should possess: An advanced university degree (Master’s level or higher) in: Public Health Health Systems Epidemiology Health Informatics Pharmacy Supply Chain Management Or another related field Equivalent combinations of education and professional experience may also be considered. Professional Experience Requirements Applicants must have: At least seven (7) years of progressively responsible professional experience Strong experience in health systems strengthening or public health programmes Experience working in low- and middle-income country settings Proven collaboration with Ministries of Health Experience supporting integration of disease-specific programmes into national systems Experience in the following systems is highly valued: DHIS2 HMIS eLMIS IDSR Integrated digital health systems Desirable Qualifications WHO also values applicants with: Experience working directly with NTD programmes Monitoring and evaluation expertise Multi-country or regional assessment experience Experience documenting country case studies and best practices Knowledge of preventive chemotherapy programmes Strong analytical and report-writing skills Language Requirements Essential Applicants must demonstrate expert knowledge of at least one of the following languages: English French Portuguese Desirable Knowledge of additional UN official languages is considered an advantage. WHO Core Competencies Successful consultants are expected to demonstrate: Teamwork Communication skills Partnership building Respect for diversity and inclusion Results-oriented work ethic Contract Duration and Work Modality Key consultancy details include: Contract duration may vary between 1 and 11 months Engagements are deliverable-based Assignments may be: Remote In-country Hybrid/blended Remuneration will depend on the scope of work and available funding. Important Deadline Application Deadline: 6 July 2026 Applicants are strongly encouraged to apply early to avoid last-minute technical issues. Important Notes for Applicants Inclusion in the roster does not guarantee a contract. WHO may use this roster to identify candidates for similar future consultancies. A written assessment may be conducted during screening. Only shortlisted candidates will be contacted. WHO strongly encourages applications from women and nationals of underrepresented Member States. WHO maintains strict ethical standards and zero tolerance for harassment or abuse. Why This Opportunity Matters This consultancy roster represents an important opportunity for experienced public health professionals to contribute directly to health systems transformation across Africa. Selected experts may help countries: Improve healthcare delivery Strengthen surveillance systems Enhance access to medicines Build sustainable health financing systems Accelerate progress toward eliminating neglected tropical diseases For professionals interested in global health consulting, health systems strengthening, digital health integration, epidemiology, or international public health policy, this WHO consultancy roster provides an exceptional platform to work on high-impact regional initiatives. Learn More Apply Click HERE to learn more and apply for the WHO African Region Applications for 2026 Roster of Consultants on NTD Health Systems Integration. Explore more opportunities on OFY HERE . The post WHO African Region Opens Applications for 2026 Roster of Consultants on NTD Health Systems Integration appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: Launch Your STEM Career with NASA: Apply Now for the JPL Year-Round Internship Program 2025
summary: Aspiring scientists, engineers, and technologists—your gateway to hands-on experience at NASA s Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL) is open year-round. Whether you re pursuing an undergraduate or graduate degree in a STEM field, the JPL Year-Round Internship Program offers a unique opportunity to work alongside world-class researchers and contribute to groundbreaking space and Earth science missions. What Is the JPL Year-Round Internship Program? The Jet Propulsion Laboratory, managed by Caltech on behalf of NASA, is home to some of the most innovative planetary exploration and Earth observation missions. The JPL Year-Round Internship Program is designed to foster the next generation of leaders in science, technology, engineering, and mathematics by offering immersive, real-world experiences throughout the academic year and summer. Through this program, selected interns are paired with JPL scientists and engineers, gaining not just practical exposure but also invaluable mentorship. These projects are carefully designed to align with the interns’ academic backgrounds while contributing meaningfully to JPL’s active missions. Key Features of the Program 1. Flexible Timing Internships are available throughout the year—fall, spring, and summer—making it easier for students to integrate the experience into their academic schedules. Interns can apply for part-time or full-time positions depending on their availability and institutional policies. 2. Paid Opportunities Interns receive a weekly monetary stipend. For full-time, in-person participants whose universities are located more than 50 miles from JPL, a supplemental housing and travel allowance is also provided. 3. Enrichment Activities Beyond project work, interns can attend enrichment sessions including lectures by NASA scientists, facility tours, career development workshops, and networking events. These experiences help broaden understanding of the space sector and the science-policy interface. 4. Personalized Mentorship Each intern is matched with a mentor who supervises their work and provides guidance on both technical challenges and career planning. This one-on-one mentorship is a core component of the learning experience. Who Is Eligible? To qualify for the JPL Year-Round Internship Program, applicants must meet the following criteria: Be currently enrolled in an accredited U.S. college or university as an undergraduate or graduate student in a STEM-related program (science, technology, engineering, or mathematics). Maintain a minimum GPA of 3.0, with a preferred GPA of 3.5 or above. Be a U.S. citizen or lawful permanent resident (LPR). Be able to commit to a minimum 10-week internship for their first term. (Subsequent terms are more flexible.) Importantly, applicants must be authorized by their university to pursue off-campus, independent study or internship during the academic year or summer. How to Apply Applications are submitted online through the JPL internship portal . The process includes: Filling out the digital application form. Uploading a current resume in PDF, DOC, or TXT format. Submitting an unofficial transcript. After submission, the Education Office at JPL screens applications for eligibility. If approved, applicants become visible to potential mentors who are looking for student collaborators on specific projects. Those selected as finalists may be contacted for an interview by their prospective mentors. Offers are extended based on mutual interest and project fit. Why Apply? Participating in the JPL Year-Round Internship Program is not just an educational milestone—it’s a career-launching opportunity. Interns become part of the NASA mission, gaining insight into space exploration, Earth science, and cutting-edge robotics. They work on meaningful projects that have the potential to fly on spacecraft, influence mission design, or impact how we understand our planet and beyond. Past interns have contributed to missions like Mars Perseverance, Europa Clipper, and Earth-observing satellites. Many have transitioned into full-time roles at NASA centers or continued their studies with stronger research portfolios and networks. to learn more and apply, click here Discover more global internship opportunities on OFY, VISIT HERE The post Launch Your STEM Career with NASA: Apply Now for the JPL Year-Round Internship Program 2025 appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: European Commission Opens Applications for the RAISE High-Level Academic Advisory Board 2026: Join Europe’s Premier AI Science Expert Group and Shape the Future of Artificial Intelligence Research
summary: The European Commission has officially launched a prestigious call for applications for the selection of members of the RAISE High-Level Academic Advisory Board , a newly established expert group designed to guide the future of Artificial Intelligence (AI) science in Europe. This exceptional opportunity invites leading AI researchers and academics from around the world to contribute directly to European policymaking, scientific strategy, and the development of the ambitious Resource for AI Science in Europe (RAISE) initiative. Selected experts will play a critical role in shaping Europe’s AI research ecosystem, influencing funding priorities, supporting scientific innovation, and providing strategic advice on the integration of artificial intelligence across scientific disciplines. Application Deadline: 4 September 2026 What is RAISE? RAISE (Resource for AI Science in Europe) is a flagship initiative being developed by the European Commission to accelerate the advancement and adoption of Artificial Intelligence in scientific research and industrial innovation. The initiative is rooted in the European Commission’s: European AI in Science Strategy (COM(2025) 724 final) Apply AI Strategy (COM(2025) 723 final) RAISE aims to create a virtual institute that brings together: World-class AI talent Advanced computing resources High-value datasets Research funding opportunities Scientific collaboration networks By pooling these resources, RAISE seeks to strengthen Europe’s leadership in AI-driven scientific discovery and technological innovation. About the RAISE High-Level Academic Advisory Board In March 2026, the European Commission s: Directorate-General for Research and Innovation (DG RTD) Directorate-General for Communications Networks, Content and Technology (DG CNECT) established the RAISE High-Level Academic Advisory Board. The Board serves as an independent advisory body composed of leading academics and experts in AI science. The Commission is seeking to appoint between 10 and 15 members who possess outstanding scientific credentials and extensive experience in AI research. Members will provide independent scientific advice to support the design, implementation, and long-term success of RAISE. Responsibilities of Selected Experts Members of the Advisory Board will support the European Commission by: 1. Advising on AI Science Policy Experts will assist DG RTD and DG CNECT in preparing policy initiatives related to: Fundamental AI research AI-enabled scientific discovery AI applications across disciplines Emerging scientific opportunities 2. Supporting the Development of RAISE The Board will provide recommendations regarding: Governance structures Strategic priorities Scientific roadmaps Operational frameworks that will help establish RAISE as a world-leading AI science platform. 3. Facilitating Knowledge Exchange Members will: Share expertise and best practices Promote collaboration among researchers Encourage interdisciplinary innovation Support international scientific cooperation 4. Producing Policy Briefs and Working Papers The European Commission may request the Board to prepare: Strategic reports Technical analyses Policy recommendations Expert position papers on AI science-related issues. Key Areas of Advice The Advisory Board may be consulted on a wide range of topics including: Research Funding Priorities Experts may advise on: Horizon Europe work programmes Research and innovation partnerships Funding priorities Emerging scientific challenges suitable for AI solutions AI Research Directions The Board may identify: Promising AI research areas Priority scientific domains Emerging technologies Areas suitable for increased or reduced investment Organizational Design of RAISE Members may provide guidance on: Institutional structures Scientific governance Academic-industry collaboration Stakeholder engagement Computing and Data Infrastructure Experts may recommend: Computing requirements Data priorities AI safety measures Infrastructure investments needed to achieve RAISE s objectives. Benchmarks and Evaluation Metrics The Board may help develop: Scientific performance indicators AI benchmarking systems Evaluation frameworks Success metrics for RAISE Composition of the Board The Advisory Board will consist of: 10–15 experts Individual members appointed in a personal capacity Independent advisors acting in the public interest Members will not represent organizations or institutions but will contribute based on their personal expertise. Term of Appointment Selected members will: Serve for two years Be eligible for renewal Remain in office until replaced or until the end of their mandate The Commission will also establish a reserve list of qualified candidates for future appointments. Meetings and Working Arrangements Members should be prepared to: Attend meetings regularly Participate actively in discussions Review documents and reports Contribute to preparatory work Serve as rapporteurs when required Expected Meeting Schedule The Board will generally meet: At least once annually in person on European Commission premises At least twice annually online Working documents and discussions will primarily be conducted in English. Financial Benefits Selected experts will receive financial compensation for their participation. Daily Allowance EUR 450 per full working day EUR 225 per half working day This compensation covers: Meeting participation Preparatory work Expert advisory activities Travel and Subsistence Coverage The European Commission will reimburse: Travel expenses Accommodation costs Subsistence expenses in accordance with Commission rules and available budget allocations. Eligibility Requirements Applicants must demonstrate: Academic Qualifications PhD degree Minimum 7 years of academic experience in AI science Scientific Excellence Evidence may include: Publications in top-tier journals and conferences Strong citation record and h-index Significant research impact Prestigious scientific awards Competitive research grants Editorial board memberships Keynote and invited conference presentations Scientific Leadership Applicants should demonstrate: Leadership roles in scientific communities Membership in professional societies Engagement with international research networks Multidisciplinary Expertise Additional value will be given to applicants who combine: Fundamental AI research Applications of AI in scientific disciplines such as health, biology, climate science, engineering, agriculture, physics, chemistry, and related fields. English Language Proficiency Applicants must possess sufficient English language skills to: Participate in meetings Contribute to discussions Draft reports and recommendations Conflict of Interest Requirements Applicants must demonstrate independence and disclose any potential conflicts of interest through a formal Declaration of Interests (DOI). The European Commission will assess all disclosures before appointments are finalized. Required Application Documents Applicants must submit: Cover Letter Classification Form (Annex I) Selection Criteria Form (Annex II) Curriculum Vitae (Europass format preferred) Declaration of Interests (Annex III) Additional supporting documents may be requested later. Application Deadline Deadline: 4 September 2026 Late applications will not be considered. Applicants are encouraged to prepare their documentation early and ensure all required forms are completed and signed before submission. Why This Opportunity Matters The RAISE High-Level Academic Advisory Board represents one of the most influential AI science advisory opportunities currently available. Selected experts will contribute directly to shaping the future of AI research policy in Europe, influencing funding decisions, scientific priorities, infrastructure development, and the integration of AI across disciplines. For distinguished researchers seeking to have a lasting impact on the future of AI-powered scientific discovery, this appointment offers a unique platform to influence policy at the highest European level while collaborating with some of the world’s leading AI experts. How to Apply Interested candidates should submit their completed and signed application package by email. Email Address: EC-RAISE@ec.europa.eu Applications may be submitted in any official EU language, though English submissions are encouraged to facilitate evaluation. Learn More For more information about this opportunity, Click here More global Job opportunities for youth, visit the OFY website: Click here The post European Commission Opens Applications for the RAISE High-Level Academic Advisory Board 2026: Join Europe’s Premier AI Science Expert Group and Shape the Future of Artificial Intelligence Research appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: Internet Society Foundation Common Good Cyber Fund 2026: Up to USD $300,000 in Multi-Year Grants for Nonprofit Cybersecurity Organizations Worldwide
summary: The Internet Society Foundation has officially opened applications for the 2026 Common Good Cyber Fund (CGCF), a global grant program designed to strengthen nonprofit organizations that protect the public-interest cybersecurity ecosystem. The initiative provides significant multi-year funding to organizations working to secure vulnerable communities, strengthen cybersecurity infrastructure, and build a safer, more resilient Internet. For organizations focused on cybersecurity, digital rights, online safety, civil society protection, or Internet resilience, this funding opportunity represents one of the most significant international grant programs currently available. Applications are now open, and eligible nonprofit organizations from around the world are encouraged to apply before the official deadline. About the Common Good Cyber Fund The Common Good Cyber Fund (CGCF) was launched in 2025 through a collaboration between the Internet Society Foundation, the Common Good Cyber Ecosystem Committee, and a Strategic Advisory Committee. The fund was created in response to the increasing frequency and sophistication of cyber threats affecting vulnerable communities across the globe. Today, cyberattacks target: Nonprofit organizations Journalists Human rights defenders Civil society organizations Independent media Dissidents High-risk Internet users Underserved communities Despite playing an essential role in protecting online civic space, many nonprofit cybersecurity organizations remain severely underfunded. The Common Good Cyber Fund was established to address this challenge by providing sustainable financial support to organizations that strengthen cybersecurity for the public good. Who Funds the Common Good Cyber Fund? The fund brings together financial contributions from multiple international partners, including: Australian Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade Craig Newmark Philanthropies United Kingdom Foreign, Commonwealth Development Office German Federal Foreign Office Global Affairs Canada Internet Society Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Kingdom of the Netherlands This collaborative funding model allows the program to support nonprofit cybersecurity organizations on a global scale. Why This Grant Matters Digital threats continue to evolve rapidly. Cyberattacks increasingly target organizations and individuals who defend democracy, human rights, and civic participation. Many organizations working to protect these communities struggle with: Limited cybersecurity resources Insufficient funding Increasingly sophisticated cyber threats Limited technical capacity Growing operational demands The Common Good Cyber Fund seeks to strengthen this ecosystem by investing directly in organizations delivering cybersecurity infrastructure, technical services, operational support, and protective programs. Funding Objectives Applicants must demonstrate that their proposed work primarily aligns with one of the three funding objectives. Objective 1: Maintenance of Critical Cybersecurity Infrastructure This objective supports the technical systems and operational capabilities that strengthen Internet security for many organizations simultaneously. Examples include: Internet infrastructure protection DNS security Routing security Secure-by-default technologies Threat detection systems Shared threat intelligence Security automation Operational coordination platforms Maintenance of open cybersecurity systems Long-term cybersecurity infrastructure improvements Projects should create lasting improvements that continue delivering value after the grant period ends. Objective 2: Delivering Scalable Cybersecurity Support for High-Risk Internet Users This objective supports organizations helping individuals and institutions facing persistent digital threats, including state-directed cyber activity and digital transnational repression. Eligible activities include: Incident response Account recovery Device triage Secure restoration services Secure onboarding Email protection Multi-factor authentication implementation Secure communications Data backup systems Managed cybersecurity services Remote technical assistance Regional responder networks Privacy-preserving information sharing The program also recognizes that technology-facilitated gender-based violence and gendered cyber harassment disproportionately affect women in public-facing roles. Organizations addressing these issues are particularly encouraged to apply. Objective 3: Building a Safer Internet for Vulnerable Communities This objective supports organizations that transform cybersecurity knowledge into safer online environments for vulnerable populations. Examples include: Evidence-based cybersecurity policy Security standards development Platform safety improvements Responsible vulnerability reporting Threat coordination mechanisms Incident response protocols Trusted reporting channels Security coordination for civil society Journalist protection initiatives Projects should demonstrate measurable improvements in Internet safety and resilience. Who Can Apply? The Common Good Cyber Fund is open to nonprofit organizations worldwide that align with the program s objectives. Eligible applicants include organizations working in areas such as: Cybersecurity Internet security Digital safety Digital rights Civil society protection Internet infrastructure Online privacy Threat intelligence Incident response Digital resilience Applicants must satisfy all eligibility requirements. Eligibility Requirements Applicants must: Be a registered nonprofit organization (U.S. 501(c)(3) or international equivalent). Focus on cybersecurity-related work. Maintain an official organizational bank account capable of receiving funds from a U.S.-based foundation. Demonstrate alignment with one or more CGCF objectives. Show evidence of delivering cybersecurity infrastructure or services that protect vulnerable communities. Demonstrate sound governance and implementation capacity. Show the ability to strengthen broader cybersecurity ecosystems. Priority Will Be Given To During the 2026 funding cycle, preference will be given to organizations that: Build cybersecurity ecosystems. Operate in Global Majority countries. Regrant funding to smaller organizations working in Global Majority regions. Promote gender equity. Address technology-facilitated gender-based violence. Support vulnerable communities. Protect journalists. Strengthen civil society organizations. Funding Available The Internet Society Foundation expects to award: At least USD $3.5 million in total funding during 2026. Individual grants will typically range between: USD $100,000 USD $300,000 Successful organizations will generally receive: Two-year operating grants Maximum total funding of USD $300,000 over the grant period Application Timeline The 2026 funding cycle follows the schedule below: Applications Open: 23 June 2026 Application Deadline: 4 August 2026 (21:00 UTC) Initial Screening: Late July 2026 External Review: Following screening Grant Decisions: Successful applicants notified after the review process Due to the anticipated number of submissions, individual feedback will not be provided to unsuccessful applicants. Required Application Documents Applicants must prepare and upload the following: Current operational budget Tax identification document Organizational governing documents List of institutional funders and funding amounts National tax returns for the two most recent fiscal years Audited financial statements for the two most recent fiscal years Optional 501(c)(3) Equivalency Determination (where applicable) Applicants are encouraged to prepare these documents well before the submission deadline. Important Registration Requirement Applications must be submitted through the Internet Society Foundation s Fluxx grants management system. Organizations should: Register in Fluxx before the application window. Complete registration at least 24 hours before the deadline . Submit applications before 4 August 2026 at 21:00 UTC . Applications submitted via email will not be accepted. Governance and Review Process The Internet Society Foundation manages: Grant administration Application review Grantee selection Grant management Reporting Strategic guidance is provided by the Global Cyber Alliance through the Strategic Advisory Committee. Applications will also be reviewed by an Independent Program Review Committee consisting of cybersecurity professionals and experts from civil society, journalism, research, and policy sectors. Additional Resources for Applicants The Foundation provides several resources to help applicants prepare competitive proposals, including: Information Session Recording Information Session Slide Deck Common Good Cyber Fund Application Guide Evaluation Framework Grantee Reporting Metrics Organizations are encouraged to review these materials carefully before submitting an application. Why Organizations Should Apply The Common Good Cyber Fund represents a major investment in strengthening nonprofit cybersecurity worldwide. Successful applicants will benefit from: Multi-year operating funding Flexible support for organizational priorities Recognition from a globally respected foundation Opportunities to strengthen cybersecurity ecosystems Increased capacity to protect vulnerable communities Support for sustainable cybersecurity infrastructure Funding for scalable, high-impact cybersecurity initiatives Organizations working to build a safer, more secure Internet should strongly consider applying before the application deadline. Apply Here For complete application details, eligibility requirements, and submission instructions, visit the Internet Society Foundation s Common Good Cyber Fund application portal. For more information, visit the official website. Discover more grant opportunities on OFY, VISIT HERE The post Internet Society Foundation Common Good Cyber Fund 2026: Up to USD $300,000 in Multi-Year Grants for Nonprofit Cybersecurity Organizations Worldwide appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: CDC Laboratory Leadership Service (LLS) Fellowship 2027 Applications Open: Fully Paid Two-Year Public Health Laboratory Leadership Program in the United States
summary: The U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) has officially opened applications for the Laboratory Leadership Service (LLS) Class of 2027, one of the most prestigious public health laboratory leadership programs in the United States. Designed to develop the next generation of laboratory leaders, the fellowship combines advanced scientific training, leadership development, public health service, and hands-on field experience. For doctoral-level laboratory scientists seeking to expand their impact beyond the laboratory bench and become influential leaders in public health, the CDC Laboratory Leadership Service Fellowship offers a unique opportunity to gain practical experience while contributing to the protection of public health and safety. What is the CDC Laboratory Leadership Service (LLS)? The Laboratory Leadership Service (LLS) is a two-year postdoctoral service-learning fellowship established by the CDC in 2015 to strengthen public health laboratory leadership, laboratory quality management, biosafety practices, and workforce development. The program was developed to create a pipeline of highly skilled laboratory professionals capable of addressing emerging public health challenges while maintaining the highest standards of laboratory excellence, quality, and safety. Unlike traditional research fellowships, LLS combines technical laboratory expertise with real-world public health applications. Fellows receive extensive training while working on critical public health issues affecting communities across the United States. Why the CDC Created the LLS Fellowship Modern public health threats require laboratory professionals who possess more than scientific expertise. Public health laboratories increasingly need leaders capable of managing laboratory systems, responding to emergencies, communicating scientific findings, and overseeing biosafety and quality assurance programs. The CDC created the LLS Fellowship to: Strengthen laboratory leadership capacity. Improve laboratory safety and quality standards. Develop future public health laboratory leaders. Support disease surveillance and outbreak investigations. Enhance emergency preparedness and response capabilities. Promote interdisciplinary collaboration between laboratory scientists and epidemiologists. What Fellows Do During the Program LLS fellows participate in a wide range of activities that provide practical experience in public health laboratory science. During the fellowship, participants: 1. Conduct Public Health Investigations Fellows work on cutting-edge laboratory investigations that support public health decision-making and disease prevention efforts. 2. Support Outbreak Responses Participants contribute to responses involving disease outbreaks, emerging health threats, disasters, and public health emergencies. 3. Gain Leadership Training The fellowship places significant emphasis on leadership development, preparing scientists to lead public health laboratories and programs. 4. Learn Laboratory Quality Management Fellows receive training in: Quality management systems Laboratory operations Quality assurance Continuous improvement strategies 5. Strengthen Biosafety Expertise Participants learn best practices in laboratory biosafety and biosecurity to ensure safe laboratory operations. 6. Develop Communication Skills The program helps fellows communicate scientific findings effectively to policymakers, health professionals, and the public. 7. Build Bioinformatics and Data Analysis Skills Participants gain experience using modern analytical tools to interpret scientific and public health data. Fellowship Training Structure The fellowship begins each July with an intensive month-long orientation and training course in Atlanta, Georgia. Throughout the two-year program, fellows receive: Hands-on laboratory training. One-on-one mentorship from CDC experts. Leadership development opportunities. Emergency response experience. Applied public health investigations. Conference presentations. Networking opportunities with leading public health professionals. Collaborative learning alongside CDC s Epidemic Intelligence Service (EIS) officers. Fellowship Benefits The CDC provides fellows with numerous professional and financial benefits. These include: Federal employment status during the fellowship. Competitive salary equivalent to GS-12 Step 3 federal pay. Locality pay adjustments based on assignment location. Federal health insurance benefits. Retirement benefits. Life insurance coverage. Dental and vision insurance. Annual leave and sick leave. Access to extensive CDC training resources. Career advancement opportunities within public health. Host Site Assignments Selected fellows may be assigned to: CDC headquarters laboratories in Atlanta. CDC laboratories located elsewhere in the United States. State public health laboratories. Territorial public health laboratories. Local public health laboratories. Fellows must be willing to relocate to their assigned location. Application Process Applicants are encouraged to carefully prepare their applications. Strong applications typically demonstrate: Interest in public health service. Leadership potential. Technical laboratory expertise. Volunteer and community service experience. Strong personal statements. Commitment to public health careers. Important Dates Application Opens June 22, 2026 Application Deadline August 2, 2026 Interview Period October 26 – November 10, 2026 Fellowship Start Date July 2027 Late applications will not be considered. Applicants are strongly encouraged to submit all required materials well before the deadline. For more information about this opportunity click here Why You Should Apply The CDC Laboratory Leadership Service Fellowship is among the premier public health laboratory leadership programs in the United States. It offers a rare opportunity to combine advanced scientific expertise with leadership development, public health service, outbreak response experience, and professional growth. For scientists who aspire to influence public health policy, strengthen laboratory systems, and lead future responses to health emergencies, the LLS Fellowship provides an exceptional pathway toward a meaningful and impactful career. For additional details and applications, visit the CDC Laboratory Leadership Service website. Deadline: August 2, 2026. Discover global fellowship opportunities on OFY here The post CDC Laboratory Leadership Service (LLS) Fellowship 2027 Applications Open: Fully Paid Two-Year Public Health Laboratory Leadership Program in the United States appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: Johns Hopkins AGI Governance Fellowship 2026 in Washington, DC: Fully Funded Elite AI Governance Program for Emerging Global Leaders
summary: As artificial intelligence continues to evolve at an unprecedented pace, governments, institutions, companies, and societies around the world face increasingly complex questions about how to govern powerful AI systems responsibly. From frontier AI models and AGI (Artificial General Intelligence) to democratic oversight, institutional design, and societal resilience, the future of governance may depend on a new generation of leaders who understand both advanced technology and public policy. To help meet this challenge, the Johns Hopkins University School of Government and Policy has launched the AGI Governance Fellowship 2026, an intensive, fully funded leadership and training program designed to prepare outstanding early-career professionals, researchers, and practitioners to contribute to the future of AI governance. Led by renowned scholars Professor Gillian Hadfield, Professor Seth Lazar, and Professor Nicholas Caputo, this highly selective fellowship brings together talented individuals from government, industry, academia, civil society, and the nonprofit sector for three weeks of immersive learning, collaboration, and problem-solving in Washington, DC. The fellowship is hosted at the Johns Hopkins University Bloomberg Center and represents a unique opportunity for emerging leaders to engage directly with some of the most pressing governance challenges posed by advanced AI systems. About the AGI Governance Fellowship The AGI Governance Fellowship is designed around a simple but urgent reality: increasingly capable AI systems will transform institutions, economies, public policy, democratic governance, and human collective decision-making. As AI systems begin to demonstrate cognitive capabilities that rival or exceed human performance in various domains, existing governance structures may prove insufficient. The fellowship seeks to equip participants with the knowledge, networks, and practical skills necessary to help society navigate the AI transition responsibly. Rather than serving as an introductory program, the fellowship functions as a capstone experience for emerging leaders who already possess a solid foundation in AI governance, frontier AI systems, public policy, law, computer science, or related fields. Why This Fellowship Matters Artificial General Intelligence presents governance challenges unlike anything humanity has previously encountered. Questions that policymakers and researchers are actively grappling with include: How should governments regulate frontier AI systems? What new institutions are needed for the AGI era? How can democratic values be embedded into AI alignment? How can societies remain resilient amid rapidly accelerating AI capabilities? What role should governments, companies, and civil society play in shaping AI development? How might AGI transform humanity s understanding of itself? The AGI Governance Fellowship creates a space where talented individuals can work together to explore these questions and develop practical solutions. What the Fellowship Offers Selected fellows will participate in an elite educational experience focused on the future of AGI governance. 1. Intensive AGI Governance Curriculum Participants will explore advanced topics including: Frontier AI legislation State and federal AI policy AGI governance frameworks Democratic approaches to AI alignment Institutional design for AGI systems Societal resilience strategies Governance of superhuman AI capabilities Public policy and emerging technologies 2. Expert-Led Learning Fellows will learn directly from: Johns Hopkins faculty Leading AI governance experts Frontier AI researchers Policymakers Government officials Civil society leaders Technology practitioners 3. Fully Funded Participation The fellowship covers: Travel to Washington, DC Accommodation Meals Incidental expenses This ensures fellows can participate fully without financial barriers. 4. Cross-Sector Networking Participants will build relationships with professionals from: Government Academia Civil society Frontier AI laboratories Technology companies Policy organizations Research institutions 5. Collaborative Project Work The fellowship concludes with group projects aimed at producing meaningful contributions to AGI governance. Topics Covered During the Fellowship The curriculum includes deep dives into critical governance questions such as: Frontier AI Legislation Participants will analyze: Federal AI legislation State-level AI regulations Emerging global governance frameworks Democratic AI Alignment Fellows will explore: Public participation in AI governance Democratic accountability Governance legitimacy Civic engagement in AI decision-making Building Societal Resilience Topics include: Economic disruption Labor market transitions Institutional adaptation Social stability amid AI transformation New Governance Institutions Participants will investigate: Future regulatory bodies Governance mechanisms for AGI Institutional innovation Cross-border governance structures Human Identity and AGI The fellowship also examines philosophical and societal implications of advanced AI systems. Illustrative Fellowship Projects Participants may work on initiatives such as: Civic AI agents supporting democratic decision-making Model AGI legislation Frontier AI evaluations Governance tools for advanced AI systems Public-interest AI governance frameworks Research on recursive self-improvement risks AI-enabled governance systems Fellowship Structure Duration The fellowship runs for three weeks. Dates September 8 – September 25, 2026 Location Johns Hopkins Bloomberg Center Washington, DC, USA Format The fellowship is conducted entirely in person. Weekly Activities Participants should expect: Faculty lectures Interactive seminars Collaborative workshops Guest speaker sessions Group projects Policy discussions Research activities Fellowship Expectations This is an intensive full-time program. Participants are expected to: Complete significant pre-reading Attend all sessions Engage actively in discussions Collaborate on projects Participate throughout the entire program The fellowship is intentionally demanding and designed for individuals serious about shaping the future of AI governance. Who Should Apply? The fellowship seeks ambitious early-career researchers and practitioners who already possess foundational expertise in AI governance. Potential applicants include: Congressional staffers Policy professionals AI company employees Researchers Academics Grantmakers Nonprofit leaders Governance specialists Public policy professionals Computer scientists Technology experts Eligibility Requirements Applicants must: Academic Qualifications Have either: A postgraduate degree in a relevant field, OR Equivalent practical experience Career Stage Be early career professionals, generally: Within eight years of receiving an undergraduate degree Knowledge Requirements Applicants should already possess: Understanding of frontier AI systems Knowledge of AI governance Technical AI knowledge Policy expertise related to AI The organizers emphasize that this is not a beginner-level transition program but rather an advanced leadership fellowship. Why You Should Apply This fellowship stands out because it: Is fully funded Provides direct access to world-class experts Focuses on one of the most important governance challenges of the century Connects fellows with leaders across sectors Offers intensive hands-on learning Builds long-term professional networks Creates opportunities to contribute meaningfully to AGI governance Application Process Interested candidates should: Review eligibility requirements. Prepare application materials. Demonstrate relevant experience in AI governance or frontier AI. Highlight leadership potential and commitment to responsible AI development. Submit an application through the Official Johns Hopkins AGI Governance Fellowship portal . Visit HERE to learn more about the Johns Hopkins AGI Governance Fellowship 2026 in Washington. Final Thoughts The Johns Hopkins AGI Governance Fellowship 2026 represents one of the most exciting opportunities available to emerging leaders working at the intersection of artificial intelligence, governance, public policy, and societal transformation. As AI systems become increasingly powerful, societies around the world will need talented professionals capable of designing governance frameworks, institutions, and policies that ensure these technologies benefit humanity. This fellowship offers a rare opportunity to learn from leading experts, collaborate with high-caliber peers, and contribute to shaping the future of AGI governance. For researchers, policymakers, technologists, and governance professionals who want to play a meaningful role in navigating the AI transition, this fully funded fellowship in Washington, DC could be a career-defining opportunity. Discover more global Fellowship and AI opportunities on OFY.
